coredata.dc:title: Dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Eu3+/C-dots@MOF hybrids for the biomarker diaminotoluene sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Si-Jia
Yan, Bing
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorescence hybrid probe Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a) has been synthesized by encapsulating the luminescent carbon dots and lanthanide ions Eu3+ into the metal-organic framework to yield the dual-emitting composite. The as-prepared fluorescent probe exhibited excellent optical properties and water-tolerance, with the potential to serve as a chemical sensor. Furthermore, the as-prepared fluorescent probe is a promising dual functional sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection and recognition of diaminotoluene (TDA), a metabolite of toluene diisocyanates (TDI) that has been associated with an increased risk of occupational asthma. Herein, we developed a sensitive, selective, and non-destructive method for early diagnosis of occupational asthma through the detection of exposure biomarker of TDI. The ratio of (IC-dots/IEu) ascended with the increased TDA concentrations. Thus, the reported TDA sensing system may be a potential candidate for further development of occupational diagnostic tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emitting
Luminescent probe
Toluene diisocyanates
Biomarker

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of MIL-53, C-dots@MIL-53 (1), and Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a); (b) TEM image and (c) XRD patterns of as-prepared C-dots.
(a) The XPS spectra of pure MIL-53, C-dots@MIL-53 (1), and Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a); (b) partial enlarged spectra of O 1 s in pure MIL-53 and 1a; (c) partial enlarged spectra of Eu 3d in 1, and 1a.
Luminescence spectra of (a) C-dots; (b) 1; (c) 1a. (d) The emission spectra of Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 in the absence and presence of 0.01 mol·L−1 TDA when excited at 308 nm.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a) dispersed in various aqueous solutions. (b) Comparison of luminescence intensity of 1a with interfering substances in the absence and presence of TDA.

(a) The relative intensity of IC-dots and IEu upon addition of different concentrations of TDA. (b) Standard curve for the response of the ratiometric sensor toward the TDA in the range of 0–3 mM at room temperature.

head.ce:title: Dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Eu3+/C-dots@MOF hybrids for the biomarker diaminotoluene sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorescence hybrid probe Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a) had been synthesized to detect the biomarker diaminotoluene (TDA).

A novel ratiometric fluorescence hybrid probe Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a) has been synthesized by encapsulating the luminescent carbon dots and lanthanide ions Eu3+ into the metal-organic framework to yield the dual-emitting composite. The as-prepared fluorescent probe exhibited excellent optical properties and water-tolerance, with the potential to serve as a chemical sensor. Furthermore, the as-prepared fluorescent probe is a promising dual functional sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection and recognition of diaminotoluene (TDA), a metabolite of toluene diisocyanates (TDI) that has been associated with an increased risk of occupational asthma. Herein, we developed a sensitive, selective, and non-destructive method for early diagnosis of occupational asthma through the detection of exposure biomarker of TDI. The ratio of (IC-dots/IEu) ascended with the increased TDA concentrations. Thus, the reported TDA sensing system may be a potential candidate for further development of occupational diagnostic tool.

ce:section.ce:para: Toluene diisocyanate (TDI) is an industrially important aliphatic compound, which is widely used for the manufacture of flexible and rigid foams, fibers, coatings, and elastomers. Over the past few years, it has been increasingly used in the automotive industry and building insulation [1]. However, TDI is a powerful irritant to the mucous membranes of the eye and respiratory tract, which is one of the most frequent causes of occupational asthma [2]. Exposure to TDI has been shown to cause adverse effects in human, thus be considered as an environmental and occupational health concern due to accumulating evidence of significant association with asthma on exposure to this aromatic material [2,3]. Although some techniques are available to monitor the workplace exposure to TDI, measurement of TDI in the air remains the most commonly used methods of determining the exposure. However, environmental monitoring of the air is complicated and requires the application of different sampling methods [4]. Moreover, due to the individual differences in breathing rates, metabolism, absorption and deposition, external assessment is insufficient to reflect the actual concentration in human owing to the multiple routes of external exposure [5]. Considering these issues, biological monitoring, which allows accurate quantification of the pollutant in human and reflects systemic, rather than external exposure, is a method of choice for exposure and health-risk assessment [6]. Furthermore, studies have reported significant correlations between air levels of TDI and the corresponding biomarker levels in urine, both in experimental and real environment monitoring for TDI [7,8]. Diaminotoluene (TDA), a detectable metabolite of TDI in urine, has often been used as a reliable urinary biomarker of exposure to evaluate all sources of TDI. The German biological tolerance (BAT) threshold limit value for TDI is 10 μg·g−1 while the American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) is working on an index for TDI [8–10]. Several efforts have been undertaken to develop efficient methods to determine the TDA in the medical field, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [1,11]. These methods are accurate; however, techniques are complicated, time-consuming and require toxic reagents. Therefore, a reliable, rapid and convenient method for the simple accurate quantification of exposure is urgently needed for workers.

The unique properties of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have encouraged extensive studies due to their wide range of potential applications including gas storage [12], catalysis [13], molecular separation, sensing [14] and diagnostics [15] in biomedicine. Notably, the luminescence properties of lanthanide MOFs in sensing have fascinated increasing interest in recent years. Luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) have been extensively studied for their unique optical properties, which enable their applications in sensing, including ions [16,17], small molecules [18–20], vapors [21] and biomarkers [22]. Nonetheless, LMOFs have certain limitations such as poor thermal and chemical stability [23], which restrict their further application to a large extent [24]. Therefore, the MOF hybrid composites, exploring the benefits of both exceptional properties of MOFs and different functionality from nanoparticles, have been investigated that may overcome the drawbacks.

Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) are an important class of carbon-containing fluorescent nanoparticles that have gained significant attention in the past decade due to their lox-toxicity, physicochemical stability, and interesting fluorescent properties [25]. The C-dots offer numerous advantages compared with other nanocarbons (fullerenes, nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes), including their ultrafine sizes, tunable functionalities at the surface and the simple synthetic routes [26,27]. Therefore, the encapsulation of C-dots with high luminescence into MOFs while substantially maintaining the original frameworks and properties of the host MOF (C-dots@MOFs) is an important method to fabricate excellent composites which exhibit superior properties to the individual components [28]. The C-dots contain many carboxylic acid moieties at their surface that may combine with the metal ions and further embed in the structure of MOFs [23,29,30].

In the present study, we have developed an effective strategy to encapsulate the nanoparticles C-dots within a readily assembled MOF material, MIL-53-COOH, in a controllable manner. After doping the Eu3+ into the hybrid, the as-obtained Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 composites comprise the advantage of lanthanide luminescence of the MOF matrix, together with the characteristic luminescence of the nanoparticles C-dots. The novel dual-emitting was found to exhibit selectivity towards TDA, a biomarker for occupational exposure to TDI. Furthermore, MOFs by virtue of their high porosity character can pre-concentrate the analyte and significantly intensify the sensing signal. Biological monitoring for isocyanates has been available for many years; however, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to describe a dual-emissive sensor based on metal-organic framework for efficient and highly sensitive detection of trace amounts of TDA in the simulated human urine.

All chemicals used were commercially available organic reagents and were of reagent grade. Unless otherwise specified, reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water (DI-water) was used throughout the experiments. AlCl3·6H2O and 1,2,4-benezene tricarboxylic acid were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid and ethylenediamine were used to synthesize C-dots. EuCl3·6H2O was synthesized according to the procedure described in the literature.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Bruker D8 (Karlsruhe, Germany) diffractometer, operated at 40 kV and 40 mA, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The diffraction pattern was measured over the angular range of 5–50 degree (2θ) with 0.02 °/step. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments were measured at 77 K on a TRISTAR 3020 system. Before the measurement, the samples were degassed at 100 °C for 5 h. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on BWS003 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on Edinburgh FLSP 920 luminescence spectrophotometer using 450 W Xenon lamp as a source of excitation with an appropriate cut-off filter. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on a Nexus 912 AO445 spectrophotometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 at room temperature by using the KBr disk method. High-resolution (HRTEM) images were recorded with a JEOL-2010 F at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The ratio of Eu3+ and Al3+ were determined by using X-7 series inductively coupled plasma-atomic absorption spectrometer (ICP-AES, Thermo Elemental, Cheshire, UK) after the nanostructure was decomposed with concentrated nitric acid. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was carried out with a JSM-5160LV-Vantage type energy spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an RBD upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Mg Ka radiation (hν = 1253.6 eV).

The luminescent C-dots nanoparticles were synthesized using a simple one-step hydrothermal method [31]. Briefly, citric acid (1.0507 g) and ethylenediamine (335 μL) were dissolved in distilled water (10 mL) to form a transparent solution. The solution was then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave (23 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The initially color-less liquid turned into a dark-brown liquid, which indicated the formation of C-dots. The aqueous solutions were purified through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 24 h to remove the small molecules from the as-prepared C-dots solutions. The aqueous solution of the C-dots was purified in a centrifuge (3000 r·min−1, 20 min) to remove the large or agglomerated particles. Dry C-dots were obtained by lyophilization of the resulting colored (brown) aqueous solution. Notably, all the initial materials used in the C-dots synthesis were non-toxic and harmless.

The MIL-53-COOH was synthesized using a hydrothermal method as described previously [32]. The C-dots were introduced into the MIL-53-COOH to form C-dots@MIL-53-COOH (1) nanoparticles according to the synthesis method as previously described for CDs@ZIF-8 [33]. A reaction mixture of the composition 0.3016 g of AlCl3·6H2O, 0344 g of H2BDC-COOH, 100 mg of as-synthesized C-dots and 15 mL of DMF was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor after stirring for 30 min at room temperature and then heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting brown powder was washed with DMF and DI-water several times to eliminate the excess of unreacted species and then dried under vacuum for 6 h at 80 °C.

The 100 mg C-dots@MIL-53-COOH was added to a 25 mL Pyrex tube containing 10 mL EuCl3·6H2O alcoholic solution (0.01 mol L−1) [34]. The resultant Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 was washed three times with ethanol to remove the excess of EuCl3·6H2O. The product was then dried at 80 °C for 6 h under vacuum [35].

In the present study, we developed a simple, rapid, and cost-effective one-step hydrothermal process to synthesize the luminescent C-dots. The structure, morphology, and characteristics of the C-dots nanoparticles were confirmed by UV–vis, FTIR, HRTEM, and XPS analysis. Fig. 1 b presented TEM image of C-dots with average particle diameter under 5 nm, indicated that the as-synthesized C-dots were uniform and monodispersed. The XRD patterns (Fig. 1c) revealed one broad peak centered around 25°, with an interlayer distance of about 3.4 Å, as calculated by using the Bragg’s law, which can be attributed to the disordered carbon atoms [31,36]. The surface functional groups of the C-dots were determined by FT-IR (Fig. S1). The absorption bands from 3100 to 3500 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching mode of OH, while the absorption bands at 1700 cm−1 specified the presence of carbonyl (CO). All the functional groups contributed improving the thermal stability of the C-dots in an aqueous system according to the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). There was minimal weight loss upon heating to 200 °C as revealed by the TG curve (Fig. S2).

The C-dots@MIL-53-COOH (1) was synthesized by directly mixing C-dots, AlCl3·6H2O, and H2BDC-COOH in the DMF. As shown in Fig. 1a, most peaks from MOF were well preserved when the powder XRD patterns of C-dots@MIL-53-COOH was compared to the simulated data from single-crystal. There was no sign of C-dots were observed on this material, which can be attributed to the low content of C-dots in this sample and as well as because C-dots were well dispersed in DMF and embedded in the skeleton of MOF during synthesis procedure. The N2 sorption isotherm of 1 was presented in Fig. S3. The Brunauer-Emmer-Teller (BET) surface areas were calculated to be 346 m2 g−1 and exhibited superior properties of gas storage capacity than the pure MOF. However, the position of C-dots in MIL matrix could not be revealed from TEM images owing to the ultrafine sizes of the C-dots. The TEM images (Fig. S4) of a crushed shell indicated the cuboid structure of the particles. Elemental analysis of the as-synthesized crystal showed the mass concentration of C, O, Al, and N was 49.26, 36.56, 10.09 and 4.09%, respectively. Taken together, these findings confirmed that the hybrid material 1 was formed through introduction C-dots into MIL-53−COOH.

No significant changes in the powder XRD pattern were observed after doping the Eu3+ into the C-dots@MIL-53−COOH, which demonstrated that the structure was remarkably robust. As presented in Fig. S5, the morphology of the Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a) revealed that the product comprised of rectangular solid shape that is in agreement with the pure MOFs. Fig. S1 depicted the FT-IR spectra of the 1 and the 1a. The bands at 3000-2500 cm−1 indicated the presence of strong hydrogen bonds with water (−OH) and carboxylic acids. The absorptions at 1130 and 778 cm-1 could be attributed to the C–H deformation vibrations of the aromatic ring, whereas the symmetric ring-stretching vibration emerged at 1497 cm-1. The peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of carboxylate coordinated to the cations; however, the vibration at 1720 cm−1 was assigned to the non-coordinated functions [37]. Furthermore, the free carboxyl groups coordinated with lanthanide ions and served as “antenna” for sensitizing the lanthanide cations’ luminescence (Fig. S6). XPS had also confirmed the coordination between the non-coordinating COOH groups and the loaded Eu ions. As shown in Fig. 2 , the O 1 s in the hybrid exhibited a higher (530.5 eV) binding energy compared to the 1, while the Eu 4d peaks appeared at 134.3 eV were lower than the Eucl3 (135.2 eV). This phenomenon could be attributed to the formation of Eu-O coordination bonds between free carboxyl and the Eu3+. To further determine the loaded concentration of Eu3+ of the 1a, ICP-AES analysis of Al/Eu ratio was measured as 9.17, indicating the existence of Eu3+. The EDS analysis of the resulting 1a proved that Eu3+ was successfully incorporated with the noncoordinating carboxyl group (Fig. S7). Gas sorption measurement confirmed the permanent porosity of the 1a. Conversely, with the successful introduction of Eu3+ into the pores, the BET surface area decreased to 233 m2·g−1. Together these measurements suggested no dramatic structural changes in the 1a. Thus permanent porosity furnishes MOFs as suitable host matrix to constrain analyte-sensor distances and can pre-concentrate the analyte apparently by virtue of the pre-concentration effect [38]. Further, the TG curve of the activated 1a was more stable than pure MOF at the higher temperature. The TG curves exhibited three significant weight-loss steps. The first weight loss occurred at 150 °C associated with the removal of incorporated water molecules. This step was followed by the decomposition of partial carboxylic acid groups between 150–400 °C. Lastly, the structure completely collapsed [32].

Next, to determine the effect of whether the encapsulation on the luminescence properties of the system, the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots, pure MOF, 1, and 1a were examined at room temperature. Although upon excitation at 350 nm, the C-dots exhibited strong emission at 450 nm (Fig. 3 ), the pure MOF showed a significantly weak emission at 386 nm upon excitation at 338 nm. The C-dots@MIL-53-COOH displayed emission band peaked at 450 nm under the excitation at 350 nm, which was consistent with C-dots; however, overall attributed to the weak emission of the matrix MOF. After successful doping Eu3+ into the hybrid, as expected, Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 hybrid simultaneously exhibited both the emissions of C-dots and the characteristic emission peaks attributed to (5D0→7FJ, J = 0–4) transitions of Eu3+ ions at 579, 591, 616, 650, and 700 nm, respectively when excited at 308 nm A significantly strong red emission at 616 nm, the absolute characteristic of the 5D0→7F2 transitions of Eu3+ was used in the sensing study. Besides, the absolute quantum yield of solid sample was determined with integrated sphere instruments. The quantum yield was about 16%, indicating that ligands can act as an antenna to efficiently transfer energy to the lanthanide ions. The relative luminescence intensity of the C-dots to Eu3+ can be adjusted by selectively altering the content of the C-dots during the synthesis.

Further, due to the presence of other primary metabolites in urine-which might interfere with detection of TDA-it is crucial to analyze the specificity of 1a. Therefore, the changes in fluorescent emission intensities of the interfering substances in the simulated urine were also determined. In order to mimic the constituents of the urine, glucose, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, NH4Cl, creatinine (Cre), creatine, urea and uric acid were prepared as aqueous solutions in 10 mmol·L−1 and assumed that uric acid was saturated solution. To investigate the selectivity and to establish the performance the performance of 1a toward the interferences in the simulated urine, the fluorescent emission responses of 1a suspensions were recorded in the presence of upper limit concentrations of these major constituents of the urine (Fig. 4 a). The ratio of intensities of the IC-dots /IEu was used for the calculation of the intensity change. Thus, combining two luminescent centers into one system can provide a self-calibration by comparing the luminescence intensities of the lanthanide ions and the carbon dots without any external reference [39]. Notably, 1a exhibited higher selectivity for TDA over the other analytes. Besides, the other substances showed negligible effects on fluorescence intensity. Slightly increased relative intensities were observed with Cre and creatine solutions as compared with other constituents; however, the TDA exhibited distinct reverse change trend. Furthermore, the lifetimes of 5D0→7F2 of the 1a were also measured by pulsed flush lamp. As presented in Table S1, the lifetime of Eu3+ in TDA solution was significantly decreased compared to the other substances’ solutions. The shorted emission lifetime and the quenching effect of the 5D0→7F2 indicated that the TDA might weekly coordinate to the lanthanide sites. After immersing in different constituent solutions, the probe was collected and dried at 60 °C. Then, the PXRD patterns and the FT-IR spectra of the powder were observed and found to be consistent with the original samples; these results indicated that the frameworks were stable and integrated into aqueous (Fig. S8 and S9).

Accurate, noninvasive, and self-referenced measurements for the detection of the occupational disease were highly desired in the fields of nanotechnology. To assess the potential of Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 as a ratiometric sensor, the concentration-dependent photoluminescent properties were investigated (Fig. 5 a). Noticeably, with the increase of the concentration of TDA, the luminescence intensity substantially decreased (Fig. S10). Fig. 5b depicted the ratio of the two luminescence intensity at 440 nm (C-dots) and 616 nm (Eu3+) versus the concentrations of TDA, normalized to this intensity ratio at an aqueous solution without TDA [40]. The experiments are repeated three times, and the error bar has been present simultaneously. A concentration-dependent curve was fitted with the relative intensity at different concentrations of TDA and the adequate linear relationship was retained, which can be computed as an equation, IC-dots/IEu = 10.92 × C + 0.607 (R2 = 0.990). According to the above equation and 3σ/slope, the LOD is calculated to be as 6.8 μg mL−1.A table has been created for the comparison the other method with our probe (Table S2). The quenching effect of TDA on the luminescence intensity of 1a was further examined by emission lifetime of Eu3+ at 616 nm. As shown in Table S3, the lifetime of 1a was significantly reduced with the increased concentration of TDA. This observation was in agreement with the luminescence response of 1a toward the TDA. The FT-IR spectra of 1a after treatment with different concentrations of TDA were also measured in Fig. S11.

The TDI has been classified as sensitizer and considered a major cause of asthma, even lower concentration can induce functional changes in humans, particularly inflammatory processes in the lung tissues. Considering the higher absorption than inhalation and additional skin adsorption of TDI, efficient biomonitoring of the corresponding metabolite TDA in urine had been considered as an essential internal exposure. Therefore, the rapid detection of urine TDA levels could represent a promising approach to diagnose the occupational disease during its early stages. Since the human urine is composed of 97% water, water toleration is an important indicator for a competent fluorescent sensor. Thus the structural and luminescent stability was further investigated after treating with different concentration of aqueous solution for different time. The XRD patterns (Fig. S12) and the luminescent spectra (Fig. S13, Table S4) were measured and recorded after 4, 8, 12 and 24 h, respectively, storage in water. The PXRD demonstrated no considerable change in the intensity and position of peaks. A slight decrease in the luminescence intensity of the complex was observed; however, the fluctuation was less than 2% within the acceptable range. The FT-IR spectra revealed negligible effects on functional groups (Fig. S14). Further, as the pH values of the urine are within the normal physiological pH range and varies from 4.5 to 8, the stability of Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 for detecting TDA within that range was an important consideration [41]. Therefore, the luminescent spectra of compound 1a suspended in different pH aqueous solutions were monitored, which demonstrated no noticeable change in the fluorescence intensity with the differences in the pH values (Fig. S15) [42]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S16) and FT-IR (Fig. S17) revealed that the skeleton of the immersed hybrids were maintained and no significant changes were observed, which were consistent with the previous studies [31,43]. The lifetime of the 1a in different pH solutions was also measured (Table S5).

To scrupulously investigate the luminescence quenching was caused by the biomarker and not by other constituents, the competition experiments were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the ratio of intensity in the absence and presence of TDA were recorded for better valuation. The quenching effect of the TDA into the solution demonstrated that the concomitant components did not influence the sensor. This further confirmed that the sensor was highly selective and sensitive toward the biomarker TDA.

The results of the present study demonstrated that the intensity of IEu = 616 nm was independent of the pH value and other interferences, and only correlated with to TDA concentration. The intensity of C-dots at 450 nm remained unchanged neither pH value nor the content of TDA. Therefore, Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 was a self-calibrated fluorescent indicator and did not require additional calibration for luminescence intensity [44,45]. To further verify the reliability and feasibility of the method, a simulated urine sample (containing glucose, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, NH4Cl, Cre, Creatine, urea and uric acid, 0.1 mmol L−1) was used to determinate after spiked with TDA. Nevertheless, the recoveries were more than 80% (Table S6).

In the present study, we tentatively deduced the potential mechanism. The sensing in the work is obtained through quenching. As confirmed by the PXRD pattern in Fig. S7, the dried powder of 1a after treatment with TDA and other interfering constituents of the urine were measured, which indicated that the quenching was not caused by the collapse of the framework. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. S18) also revealed that the analyte and the sensor had no overlap in the absorption range and demonstrated that the competitive absorption of the source energy between the sensor and the biomarker could be excluded. To further elucidate the mechanism of the luminescent sensor, the luminescence lifetime of the 1a in different solutions were recorded. The dynamic quenching exhibited a comparable decrease in a lifetime; however, static quenching did not have any influence on the lifetime [46]. Table S2 represented the fluorescence lifetime of 1a with different TDA concentrations. The lifetimes upon addition of TDA decreased continuously, and the reduction in a lifetime was proportional to the concentration, which suggested that the collision of the probe and analyte intensified the non-radiative deactivation through the vibrations of TDA (NH) and led to the reduction of luminescence [47]. The fluorescence signal of the 1a could not be recovered after it was quenched in TDA solution, which could be attributed to the weak interaction between the TDA and Eu3+ [48].

In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescence hybrid probe has been synthesized by encapsulating the luminescent carbon dots and the lanthanide ions into metal-organic framework to yield the dual-emitting Eu3+/C-dots@MIL-53 (1a). Furthermore, by varying the molar content of the C-dots embedded in the MOFs, the composite 1a exhibited the characteristic emission bands of both C-dots and Eu3+ ions. The as-prepared fluorescent probe is a promising dual functional sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection and recognition of TDA, even in the presence of major components of the urine. Moreover, the probe not only exhibited a high water-tolerance but also showed acid-base stability, which is essential for the practical application. Our findings indicated that the 1a exhibited low toxicity and could accurately and selectively sense trace amounts of TDA in the simulated urine. These results suggest a new route to create luminescent hybrid material that might enable early rapid detection of disease or be applicable for other sensing. In conclusion, we report the self-referencing and dual-emitting probe that is accurate and highly selective in detecting the exposure biomarker, which can be rationally designed to serve as a potential practical sensor for sensing TDA in human urine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21571142) Developing Science Funds of Tongji University, and the Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (14DZ2261100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.06.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occupational exposure to diisocyanates in polyurethane foam factory workers
Int. J. Occup. Med. Environ. Health

Exhaled nitric oxide dynamics in asthmatic reactions induced by diisocyanates
Monoclonal antibodies against toluene diisocyanate haptenated proteins from vapor-exposed mice
Biomonitoring of 2,4’ -methylene diphenyldianiline for assessment of exposure to methylene diphenyl diisocyanate aerosol
Biomarkers of exposure in monday morning urine samples as a long-term measure of exposure to aromatic diisocyanates
Upper reference limits for biomarkers of exposure to aromatic diisocyanates
Biological monitoring of exposure to toluene diisocyanate

An amine-functionalized MIL-53 metal-organic framework with large separation power for CO2 and CH4
Incorporation of molecular catalysts in metal-organic frameworks for highly efficient heterogeneous catalysis
Enhanced ligand-based luminescence in metal-organic framework sensor
An updated roadmap for the integration of metal-organic frameworks with electronic devices and chemical sensors
Assembly of framework-isomeric 4d4f heterometallic metal-organic frameworks with neutral/anionic micropores and guest-tuned luminescence properties
Facile synthesis l-cysteine capped CdS: Eu quantum dots and their Hg2+ sensitive properties
Luminescent open metal sites within a metal-organic framework for sensing small molecules
Turn-on luminescence sensing and real-time detection of traces of water in organic solvents by a flexible metal-organic framework
One-pot carbonization synthesis of europium-doped carbon quantum dots for highly selective detection of tetracycline
A luminescent metal-organic framework as a turn-on sensor for DMF vapor
Highly sensing probe for biological metabolite of benzene series pollutants based on recyclable Eu3+ functionalized metal-organic frameworks hybrids
Carbon nanodots functional MOFs composites by a stepwise synthetic approach: enhanced H2 storage and fluorescent sensing
Complex nanostructures from materials based on metal-organic frameworks for electrochemical energy storage and conversion
Microwave-assisted synthesis of water-soluble Eu3+ hybrid carbon dots with enhanced fluorescence for the sensing of Hg2+ ions and imaging of fungal cells
Encapsulation of strongly fluorescent carbon quantum dots in metal-organic frameworks for enhancing chemical sensing
Chemically induced fluorescence switching of carbon-dots and its multiple logic gate implementation
Fabrication and application of a ratiometric and colorimetric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ based on dual-emissive metal-organic framework hybrids with carbon dots and Eu3+
Imparting functionality to a metal-organic framework material by controlled nanoparticle encapsulation
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Thermal post-synthetic modification of Al-MIL-53-COOH: systematic investigation of the decarboxylation and condensation reaction
Carbon nanodots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 nanoparticles for simultaneous pH-responsive drug delivery and fluorescence imaging
Metal-organic frameworks: a rapidly growing class of versatile nanoporous materials

Metal-organic frameworks: functional luminescent and photonic materials for sensing applications
A bimetallic lanthanide metal-organic material as a self-calibrating color-gradient luminescent sensor
A high performance fluorescent arylamine sensor toward lung cancer sniffing
Novel Tb-MOF embedded with viologen species for multi-photofunctionality: photochromism, photomodulated fluorescence, and luminescent pH sensing
Facilely prepared carbon dots and rare earth ion doped hybrid composites for ratio-metric pH sensing and white-light emission
Novel photofunctional hybrid materials (alumina and titania) functionalized with both MOF and lanthanide complexes through coordination bonds
Dual-emitting MOF superset of dye composite for ratiometric temperature sensing
A mixed-crystal lanthanide zeolite-like metal-organic framework as a fluorescent indicator for lysophosphatidic acid, a cancer biomarker
Pre-concentration and energy transfer enable the efficient luminescence sensing of transition metal ions by metal-organic frameworks
Recyclable lanthanide-functionalized MOF hybrids to determine hippuric acid in urine as a biological index of toluene exposure
Fluorogenic and chromogenic chemosensors and reagents for anions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sijia Qin received her bachelor’s degree in engineering from the school of materials science and chemical engineering, Ningbo University in 2016. She is a master candidate in School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University. Her research interest is in the detection of biological metabolites in urine by rare earth optical functional hybrid materials based on metal-organic framework.

Dr. Bing Yan received his BSc and MSc in Applied Chemistry from Harbin Institute of Technology in 1992 and 1995, respectively. Then he studied at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, where he obtained his pH.D. in Inorganic Chemistry in 1998. In 1998–2001, he came to City University of Hong Kong to be a research assistant and then became PostD fellow in Peking University and University of Sherebrook. At the end of 2001, he joined Tongji University as a professor. His current research interests are focused on rare earth chemistry and functional materials science. At present, he has published over 400 scientific papers that has been cited more than 8000 times as corresponding author in international journals.
